width(W) :- W = 1 + #max { C : col_exists(C) }.
row_exists(R) :- input(R,_,_).
col_exists(C) :- input(_,C,_).

% leftmost non‑zero cell in each row
first(R,N) :- N = #min { C : input(R,C,Col), Col != 0 }.

% Row has a coloured cell?

% colour of that leftmost coloured cell
orig_color(R,N,Col) :- input(R,N,Col), Col != 0, first(R,N).

% colour that should appear at a cell (R,C)
desired_color(R,C,Col) :- has_nonzero(R), first(R,N), C >= N,
                      (C - N) # mod 2 = 0, orig_color(R,N,Col).
desired_color(R,C,5)   :- has_nonzero(R), first(R,N), C >= N,
                      (C - N) # mod 2 = 1.

% rows without any coloured cell keep the original colour
desired_color(R,C,V) :- not has_nonzero(R), input(R,C,V).

% a colour is allowed for a cell iff it equals the desired colour
allowed(R,C,V) :- desired_color(R,C,V).

% positions of output cells
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% exactly one colour per cell, forced to be allowed
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% reject any colour that is not the allowed one
:- output(R,C,Col), not allowed(R,C,Col).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule:
%   In each row, after the leftmost coloured cell (colour C),
%   the cells to the right are filled by alternating colours:
%   – the immediate neighbour gets the fixed filler colour 5,
%   – the next cell gets the original colour C,
%   – then 5, C, … alternating.
%   Rows that contain no coloured cell keep their original colours.
%   The filler colour 5 is used in all examples.
% --------------------------------------------------------------
% Encoding strategy:
%   Strategy A (direct derivation) is used: for each output cell
%   we compute the unique colour that must appear according to the rule.
%   The allowed colour for a cell is expressed by the auxiliary predicate
%   allowed(R,C,V).  The 1 {output(R,C,Col) : color(Col)} pattern
%   together with the constraint ¬allowed(R,C,Col) forces the solver to
%   pick exactly that colour – no choices remain.
%   Dimensions are derived from the input using #max and #min.
% --------------------------------------------------------------
% Edge conditions:
%   * A row without any non‑zero cell keeps the original colour (0 in the
%     training data).  This is captured by the clause
%     desired_color(R,C,V) :- not has_nonzero(R), input(R,C,V).
%   * The parity check (C‑N) \ 2 = 0 / 1 guarantees the alternating
%     pattern; it is expressed with the safe “\” arithmetic operator.
%   * The puzzle never contains more than one coloured cell per row, so
%     the leftmost coloured cell uniquely determines the whole row.

% Determine height and width
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : row_exists(R) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : col_exists(C) }.
row_exists(R) :- input(R,_,_).
col_exists(C) :- input(_,C,_).

% leftmost non‑zero column in a row
first(R,N) :- N = #min { C : input(R,C,Col), Col != 0 }.

% does the row contain a coloured cell?
has_nonzero(R) :- first(R,N).

% colour of the leftmost coloured cell
orig_color(R,N,Col) :- input(R,N,Col), Col != 0, first(R,N).

% desired colour according to the rule
desired_color(R,C,Col) :- has_nonzero(R), first(R,N), C >= N,
                      (C - N) \ 2 = 0, orig_color(R,N,Col).
desired_color(R,C,5)   :- has_nonzero(R), first(R,N), C >= N,
                      (C - N) \ 2 = 1.

% rows without a coloured cell keep their original colour
desired_color(R,C,V) :- not has_nonzero(R), input(R,C,V).

% a colour is allowed for a cell iff it equals the desired colour
allowed(R,C,V) :- desired_color(R,C,V).

% positions of output cells
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% exactly one colour per cell, forced to be allowed
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% reject any colour that is not the allowed one
:- output(R,C,Col), not allowed(R,C,Col).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule:
%   In each row, after the leftmost coloured cell (colour C),
%   the cells to the right are filled by alternating colours:
%   – the immediate neighbour gets colour 5,
%   – the next cell gets colour C,
%   – then 5, C, … alternating.
%   Rows without a coloured cell keep their original colour.
%   The filler colour is always 5 (as in the training data).
% --------------------------------------------------------------
% Why the encoding works:
%   * height and width are derived from the input using #max.
%   * first(R,N) finds the leftmost non‑zero column in a row.
%   * has_nonzero(R) records rows that contain a colour.
%   * desired_color(R,C,V) encodes exactly the colour that must appear at
%     position (R,C).  It distinguishes three zones:
%        – columns before the seed use the original colour (input(R,C,V)).
%        – columns on the right of the seed use the parity rule:
%          even offset → original colour, odd offset → filler 5.
%   * allowed(R,C,V) simply copies desired_color.
%   * output_cell(R,C) enumerates all output positions.
%   * 1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C)
%     forces exactly one colour per cell.
%   * The constraint ¬allowed(R,C,Col) eliminates every colour that is not
%     the desired one, leaving only the unique solution.
% --------------------------------------------------------------
% Edge cases:
%   – A row with no coloured cell uses the fallback clause, so the
%     output colour is the (zero) colour that is present in the input.
%   – The parity check is written with the safe “\” operator; the rule
%     works for any integer difference.
%   – The puzzle never contains more than one coloured cell per row,
%     therefore the leftmost seed uniquely determines the whole row.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : row_exists(R) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : col_exists(C) }.
row_exists(R) :- input(R,_,_).
col_exists(C) :- input(_,C,_).

% leftmost non‑zero column in a row (N = column index)
first(R,N) :- N = #min { C : input(R,C,Col), Col != 0 }.

% rows that contain at least one coloured cell
has_nonzero(R) :- first(R,N).

% colour of the leftmost coloured cell
orig_color(R,N,Col) :- input(R,N,Col), Col != 0, first(R,N).

% colour that must appear at (R,C) according to the rule
desired_color(R,C,V) :- has_nonzero(R), first(R,N), C < N, V = input(R,C,V).
desired_color(R,C,Col) :- has_nonzero(R), first(R,N), C >= N,
                      (C - N) \ 2 = 0, orig_color(R,N,Col).
desired_color(R,C,5)   :- has_nonzero(R), first(R,N), C >= N,
                      (C - N) \ 2 = 1.
desired_color(R,C,V) :- not has_nonzero(R), input(R,C,V).

% a colour is allowed for a cell iff it is the desired colour
allowed(R,C,V) :- desired_color(R,C,V).

% output cell positions (same dimensions as the input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% exactly one colour per cell, forced by the allowed set
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% reject any colour that is not the allowed one
:- output(R,C,Col), not allowed(R,C,Col).

```asp
% --------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule discovered in the training set:
%   In each row, after the left‑most coloured cell (colour C) the
%   cells to the right are filled by alternating colours:
%       – the immediate neighbour gets the fixed filler colour 5,
%       – the next cell gets the original colour C,
%       – then 5, C, 5, C … alternating.
%   Rows that contain no coloured cell keep their original colour
%   (which is 0 in the given examples).  The filler colour is always
%   5 (as seen in the data).
%
% Encoding strategy:
%   Strategy A (direct derivation).  For every output position we
%   compute exactly one colour that must appear there (the “desired
%   colour”).  The predicate allowed(R,C,V) holds iff V is the
%   desired colour of cell (R,C).  The choice rule
%   1{output(R,C,Col):color(Col)}1:-output_cell(R,C) picks a colour,
%   and the constraint “¬allowed(R,C,Col)” eliminates every colour
%   that is not the desired one.  Because allowed is true for exactly
%   one colour per cell, the solver has no freedom – there is exactly
%   one answer set that matches the reference output.
%
% Edge conditions handled:
%   • Rows without a coloured cell use the fallback clause, so the
%     output colour is the (zero) colour that already appears in the
%     input.
%   • The parity test (C−N) \ 2 = 0 / 1 is written with the safe “\”
%     operator; it works for any integer difference.
%   • The training puzzles never have more than one coloured cell per
%     row, therefore the left‑most coloured cell uniquely determines the
%     whole row.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% ----- grid dimensions (same as the input) -----
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : row_exists(R) }.
width (W) :- W = 1 + #max { C : col_exists(C) }.
row_exists(R) :- input(R,_,_).
col_exists (C) :- input(_,C,_).

% ----- leftmost coloured cell in each row -----
% first(R,N) is true with N the smallest column that contains a colour ≠0.
first(R,N) :- N = #min { C : input(R,C,Col), Col != 0 }.

% does the row contain a coloured cell?
has_nonzero(R) :- first(R,N).

% colour of that leftmost coloured cell
orig_color(R,N,Col) :- input(R,N,Col), Col != 0, first(R,N).

% colour that must appear at (R,C) according to the rule
desired_color(R,C,V) :- has_nonzero(R), first(R,N), C < N, V = input(R,C,V).
desired_color(R,C,Col) :- has_nonzero(R), first(R,N), C >= N,
                      (C - N) \ 2 = 0, orig_color(R,N,Col).
desired_color(R,C,5)   :- has_nonzero(R), first(R,N), C >= N,
                      (C - N) \ 2 = 1.
desired_color(R,C,V) :- not has_nonzero(R), input(R,C,V).

% a colour is allowed for a cell iff it equals the desired colour
allowed(R,C,V) :- desired_color(R,C,V).

% output cell positions (same size as the input grid)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% exactly one colour per cell, forced to be the allowed one
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% reject any colour that is not the allowed one
:- output(R,C,Col), not allowed(R,C,Col).
```

%   In each row, after the left‑most coloured cell (colour C) the
%   cells to the right are filled by alternating colours:
%       – the immediate neighbour gets the fixed filler colour 5,
%       – the next cell gets the original colour C,
%       – then 5, C, 5, C … alternating.
%   Rows that contain no coloured cell keep their original colour
%   (which is 0 in the given examples).  The filler colour is always
%   5 (as seen in the data).
%
% Encoding strategy:
%   Strategy A (direct derivation).  For every output position we
%   compute exactly one colour that must appear there (the “desired
%   colour”).  The predicate allowed(R,C,V) holds iff V is the
%   desired colour of cell (R,C).  The choice rule
%   1{output(R,C,Col):color(Col)}1:-output_cell(R,C) picks a colour,
%   and the constraint “¬allowed(R,C,Col)” eliminates every colour
%   that is not the desired one.  Because allowed is true for exactly
%   one colour per cell, the solver has no freedom – there is exactly
%   one answer set that matches the reference output.
%
% Edge conditions handled:
%   • Rows without a coloured cell use the fallback clause, so the
%     output colour is the (zero) colour that already appears in the
%     input.
%   • The parity test (C−N) \ 2 = 0 / 1 is written with the safe “\”
%     operator; it works for any integer difference.
%   • The training puzzles never have more than one coloured cell per
%     row, therefore the left‑most coloured cell uniquely determines the
%     whole row.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% ----- grid dimensions (same as the input) -----
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : row_exists(R) }.
width (W) :- W = 1 + #max { C : col_exists(C) }.
row_exists(R) :- input(R,_,_).
col_exists (C) :- input(_,C,_).

% ----- leftmost coloured cell in each row -----
% first(R,N) is true with N the smallest column that contains a colour ≠0.
first(R,N) :- N = #min { C : input(R,C,Col), Col != 0 }.

% does the row contain a coloured cell?
has_nonzero(R) :- first(R,N).

% colour of that leftmost coloured cell
orig_color(R,N,Col) :- input(R,N,Col), Col != 0, first(R,N).

% colour that must appear at (R,C) according to the rule
desired_color(R,C,V) :- has_nonzero(R), first(R,N), C < N, V = input(R,C,V).

% a colour is allowed for a cell iff it equals the desired colour

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% Fixed Clingo program (only syntax issues addressed)
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% ----- grid dimensions (computed directly, no unsafe vars) -----
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : row_exists(R) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : col_exists(C) }.

row_exists(R) :- input(R,_,_).
col_exists(C) :- input(_,C,_).

% ----- leftmost coloured cell in each row (N = column index) -----
leftmost(R,N,Col) :-
    N = #min { C : input(R,C,Col2), Col2 != 0 },
    input(R,N,Col),
    Col != 0.

% Row contains at least one coloured cell
row_has_color(R) :- leftmost(R,N,Col).

% ----- desired colour for a cell (safe: N appears positively) -----
% cells left of the leftmost coloured cell keep the original colour
desired_color(R,C,Col) :-
    row_has_color(R),
    leftmost(R,N,Col),
    C < N,
    Col = input(R,C,Col).

% cells right of the leftmost coloured cell:
%   if the distance (C‑N) is even -> colour of the leftmost cell,
%   if the distance is odd   -> filler colour 5
desired_color(R,C,Col) :-
    row_has_color(R),
    leftmost(R,N,Col),
    C >= N,
    (C - N) \ 2 = 0,
    Col = input(R,N,Col).

desired_color(R,C,5) :-
    row_has_color(R),
    leftmost(R,N,Col),
    C >= N,
    (C - N) \ 2 = 1.

% fallback: rows without coloured cells keep their input colour
allowed(R,C,Col) :-
    not row_has_color(R),
    input(R,C,Col).

% ----- a colour is allowed for a cell iff it equals the desired colour -----
allowed(R,C,Col) :- desired_color(R,C,Col).

% ----- output cell positions (same size as the input grid) -----
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% ----- exactly one colour per cell, forced to be the allowed one -----
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% ----- reject any colour that is not the allowed one -----
:- output(R,C,Col), not allowed(R,C,Col).

rounds.system_prompt:
You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

# Correct Pattern Reference

```asp
% Computing grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.
```

```asp
% --------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule discovered in the training set:
%   In each row, after the left‑most coloured cell (colour C) the
%   cells to the right are filled by alternating colours:
%       – the immediate neighbour gets the fixed filler colour 5,
%       – the next cell gets the original colour C,
%       – then 5, C, 5, C … alternating.
%   Rows that contain no coloured cell keep their original colour
%   (which is 0 in the given examples).  The filler colour is always
%   5 (as seen in the data).
%
% Encoding strategy:
%   Strategy A (direct derivation).  For every output position we
%   compute exactly one colour that must appear there (the “desired
%   colour”).  The predicate allowed(R,C,V) holds iff V is the
%   desired colour of cell (R,C).  The choice rule
%   1{output(R,C,Col):color(Col)}1:-output_cell(R,C) picks a colour,
%   and the constraint “¬allowed(R,C,Col)” eliminates every colour
%   that is not the desired one.  Because allowed is true for exactly
%   one colour per cell, the solver has no freedom – there is exactly
%   one answer set that matches the reference output.
%
% Edge conditions handled:
%   • Rows without a coloured cell use the fallback clause, so the
%     output colour is the (zero) colour that already appears in the
%     input.
%   • The parity test (C−N) \ 2 = 0 / 1 is written with the safe “\”
%     operator; it works for any integer difference.
%   • The training puzzles never have more than one coloured cell per
%     row, therefore the left‑most coloured cell uniquely determines the
%     whole row.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% ----- grid dimensions (same as the input) -----
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : row_exists(R) }.
width (W) :- W = 1 + #max { C : col_exists(C) }.
row_exists(R) :- input(R,_,_).
col_exists (C) :- input(_,C,_).

% ----- leftmost coloured cell in each row -----
% first(R,N) is true with N the smallest column that contains a colour ≠0.
first(R,N) :- N = #min { C : input(R,C,Col), Col != 0 }.

% does the row contain a coloured cell?
has_nonzero(R) :- first(R,N).

% colour of that leftmost coloured cell

% colour that must appear at (R,C) according to the rule
desired_color(R,C,V) :- has_nonzero(R), first(R,N), C < N, V = input(R,C,V).
desired_color(R,C,Col) :- has_nonzero(R), first(R,N), C >= N,
                      (C - N) \ 2 = 0, orig_color(R,N,Col).
desired_color(R,C,5)   :- has_nonzero(R), first(R,N), C >= N,
                      (C - N) \ 2 = 1.
desired_color(R,C,V) :- not has_nonzero(R), input(R,C,V).

% a colour is allowed for a cell iff it equals the desired colour
allowed(R,C,V) :- desired_color(R,C,V).

1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% reject any colour that is not the allowed one
```

```asp
% --------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule discovered in the training set:
%   In each row, after the left‑most coloured cell (colour C) the
%   cells to the right are filled by alternating colours:
%       – the immediate neighbour gets the fixed filler colour 5,
%       – the next cell gets the original colour C,
%       – then 5, C, 5, C … alternating.
%   Rows that contain no coloured cell keep their original colour
%   (which is 0 in the given examples).  The filler colour is always
%   5 (as seen in the data).
%
% Encoding strategy:
%   Strategy A (direct derivation).  For every output position we
%   compute exactly one colour that must appear there (the “desired
%   colour”).  The predicate allowed(R,C,V) holds iff V is the
%   desired colour of cell (R,C).  The choice rule
%   1{output(R,C,Col):color(Col)}1:-output_cell(R,C) picks a colour,
%   and the constraint “¬allowed(R,C,Col)” eliminates every colour
%   that is not the desired one.  Because allowed is true for exactly
%   one colour per cell, the solver has no freedom – there is exactly
%   one answer set that matches the reference output.
%
% Edge conditions handled:
%   • Rows without a coloured cell use the fallback clause, so the
%     output colour is the (zero) colour that already appears in the
%     input.
%   • The parity test (C−N) \ 2 = 0 / 1 is written with the safe “\”
%     operator; it works for any integer difference.
%   • The training puzzles never have more than one coloured cell per
%     row, therefore the left‑most coloured cell uniquely determines the
%     whole row.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% ----- grid dimensions (same as the input) -----
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : row_exists(R) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : col_exists(C) }.

row_exists(R) :- input(R,_,_).
col_exists(C) :- input(_,C,_).

% ----- leftmost coloured cell in each row -----
first(R,N) :- N = #min { C, Col : input(R,C,Col), Col != 0 }.

has_nonzero(R) :- first(R,N).

orig_color(R,N,Col) :- input(R,N,Col), Col != 0, first(R,N).

% colour that must appear at (R,C) according to the rule
desired_color(R,C,V) :- has_nonzero(R), first(R,N), C < N, V = input(R,C,V).
desired_color(R,C,Col) :- has_nonzero(R), first(R,N), C >= N,
                      (C - N) \ 2 = 0, orig_color(R,N,Col).
desired_color(R,C,5)   :- has_nonzero(R), first(R,N), C >= N,
                      (C - N) \ 2 = 1.
desired_color(R,C,V) :- not has_nonzero(R), input(R,C,V).

% a colour is allowed for a cell iff it equals the desired colour
allowed(R,C,V) :- desired_color(R,C,V).

% output cell positions (same size as the input grid)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% exactly one colour per cell, forced to be the allowed one
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% reject any colour that is not the allowed one
:- output(R,C,Col), not allowed(R,C,Col).
```

rounds.system_prompt:
You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

# Correct Pattern Reference

```asp
% Computing grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.
```

Common Clingo syntax fixes (apply immediately):
  aggregate(N=#count,cond,N)    →  N = #count { _ : cond }
  aggregate_all(#count,cond,N)  →  N = #count { _ : cond }
  C #mod 2                      →  C \ 2
  #min{...} = Var               →  Var = #min{...}
  { } exactly N :-              →  N { } N :-
  #count(...)                   →  #count{...}
  #sum(...)                     →  #sum{...}
  #const UPPER = N.             →  #const upper = N.
  X = #max{...}.  (standalone)  →  max_val(X) :- X = #max{...}.
  #count{A} = #count{B} (body)  →  N = #count{A}, N = #count{B}  (bind one side first)

```asp
% --------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule discovered in the training set:
%   In each row, after the left‑most coloured cell (colour C) the
%   cells to the right are filled by alternating colours:
%       – the immediate neighbour gets the fixed filler colour 5,
%       – the next cell gets the original colour C,
%       – then 5, C, 5, C … alternating.
%   Rows that contain no coloured cell keep their original colour
%   (which is 0 in the given examples).  The filler colour is always
%   5 (as seen in the data).
%
% Encoding strategy:
%   Strategy A (direct derivation).  For every output position we
%   compute exactly one colour that must appear there (the “desired
%   colour”).  The predicate allowed(R,C,V) holds iff V is the
%   desired colour of cell (R,C).  The choice rule
%   1{output(R,C,Col):color(Col)}1:-output_cell(R,C) picks a colour,
%   and the constraint “¬allowed(R,C,Col)” eliminates every colour
%   that is not the desired one.  Because allowed is true for exactly
%   one colour per cell, the solver has no freedom – there is exactly
%   one answer set that matches the reference output.
%
% Edge conditions handled:
%   • Rows without a coloured cell use the fallback clause, so the
%     output colour is the (zero) colour that already appears in the
%     input.
%   • The parity test (C−N) \ 2 = 0 / 1 is written with the safe “\”
%     operator; it works for any integer difference.
%   • The training puzzles never have more than one coloured cell per
%     row, therefore the left‑most coloured cell uniquely determines the
%     whole row.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% ----- grid dimensions (same as the input) -----
```
max_row(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max { R : row_exists(R) }.
height(H) :- H = 1 + Rmax.

max_col(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max { C : col_exists(C) }.
width(W) :- W = 1 + Cmax.

row_exists(R) :- input(R,_,_).
col_exists(C) :- input(_,C,_).

```asp
% ----- leftmost coloured cell in each row -----
first(R,N) :- N = #min { C, Col : input(R,C,Col), Col != 0 }.

has_nonzero(R) :- first(R,N).

orig_color(R,N,Col) :- input(R,N,Col), Col != 0, first(R,N).

% colour that must appear at (R,C) according to the rule
desired_color(R,C,V) :- has_nonzero(R), first(R,N), C < N, V = input(R,C,V).
desired_color(R,C,Col) :- has_nonzero(R), first(R,N), C >= N,
                        (C - N) \ 2 = 0, orig_color(R,N,Col).
desired_color(R,C,5)   :- has_nonzero(R), first(R,N), C >= N,
                        (C - N) \ 2 = 1.
desired_color(R,C,V) :- not has_nonzero(R), input(R,C,V).

% a colour is allowed for a cell iff it equals the desired colour
allowed(R,C,V) :- desired_color(R,C,V).

% output cell positions (same size as the input grid)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% exactly one colour per cell, forced to be the allowed one
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% reject any colour that is not the allowed one
:- output(R,C,Col), not allowed(R,C,Col).

% auxiliary counts (optional, kept for completeness)
nonzero_count(R,N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.
```

You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

```asp
% Computing grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.
```

```asp
% --------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule discovered in the training set:
%   In each row, after the left‑most coloured cell (colour C) the
%   cells to the right are filled by alternating colours:
%       – the immediate neighbour gets the fixed filler colour 5,
%       – the next cell gets the original colour C,
%       – then 5, C, 5, C … alternating.
%   Rows that contain no coloured cell keep their original colour
%   (which is 0 in the given examples).  The filler colour is always
%   5 (as seen in the data).
%
% Encoding strategy:
%   Strategy A (direct derivation).  For every output position we
%   compute exactly one colour that must appear there (the “desired
%   colour”).  The predicate allowed(R,C,V) holds iff V is the
%   desired colour of cell (R,C).  The choice rule
%   1{output(R,C,Col):color(Col)}1:-output_cell(R,C) picks a colour,
%   and the constraint “¬allowed(R,C,Col)” eliminates every colour
%   that is not the desired one.  Because allowed is true for exactly
%   one colour per cell, the solver has no freedom – there is exactly
%   one answer set that matches the reference output.
%
% Edge conditions handled:
%   • Rows without a coloured cell use the fallback clause, so the
%     output colour is the (zero) colour that already appears in the
%     input.
%   • The parity test (C−N) \ 2 = 0 / 1 is written with the safe “\”
%     operator; it works for any integer difference.
%   • The training puzzles never have more than one coloured cell per
%     row, therefore the left‑most coloured cell uniquely determines the
%     whole row.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% ----- grid dimensions (same as the input) -----
max_row(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max { R : row_exists(R) }.
height(H) :- H = 1 + Rmax.

max_col(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max { C : col_exists(C) }.
width(W) :- W = 1 + Cmax.

row_exists(R) :- input(R,_,_).
col_exists(C) :- input(_,C,_).

% ----- leftmost coloured cell in each row -----
first(R,N) :- N = #min { C, Col : input(R,C,Col), Col != 0 }.

has_nonzero(R) :- first(R,N).

orig_color(R,N,Col) :- input(R,N,Col), Col != 0, first(R,N).

% colour that must appear at (R,C) according to the rule
desired_color(R,C,V) :- has_nonzero(R), first(R,N), C < N, V = input(R,C,V).
desired_color(R,C,Col) :- has_nonzero(R), first(R,N), C >= N,
                        (C - N) \ 2 = 0, orig_color(R,N,Col).
desired_color(R,C,5)   :- has_nonzero(R), first(R,N), C >= N,
                        (C - N) \ 2 = 1.
desired_color(R,C,V) :- not has_nonzero(R), input(R,C,V).

% a colour is allowed for a cell iff it equals the desired colour
allowed(R,C,V) :- desired_color(R,C,V).

% output cell positions (same size as the input grid)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% exactly one colour per cell, forced to be the allowed one
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% reject any colour that is not the allowed one
:- output(R,C,Col), not allowed(R,C,Col).

% auxiliary counts (optional, kept for completeness)
nonzero_count(R,N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.
```

rounds.response:
```asp
% --------------------------------------------------------------
% Fixed Clingo program (only syntax issues addressed)
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% ----- grid dimensions (computed directly, no unsafe vars) -----
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : row_exists(R) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : col_exists(C) }.

row_exists(R) :- input(R,_,_).
col_exists(C) :- input(_,C,_).

% ----- leftmost coloured cell in each row (N = column index) -----
leftmost(R,N,Col) :-
    N = #min { C : input(R,C,Col2), Col2 != 0 },
    input(R,N,Col),
    Col != 0.

% Row contains at least one coloured cell
row_has_color(R) :- leftmost(R,N,Col).

% ----- desired colour for a cell (safe: N appears positively) -----
% cells left of the leftmost coloured cell keep the original colour
desired_color(R,C,Col) :-
    row_has_color(R),
    leftmost(R,N,Col),
    C < N,
    Col = input(R,C,Col).

% cells right of the leftmost coloured cell:
%   if the distance (C‑N) is even -> colour of the leftmost cell,
%   if the distance is odd   -> filler colour 5
desired_color(R,C,Col) :-
    row_has_color(R),
    leftmost(R,N,Col),
    C >= N,
    (C - N) \ 2 = 0,
    Col = input(R,N,Col).

desired_color(R,C,5) :-
    row_has_color(R),
    leftmost(R,N,Col),
    C >= N,
    (C - N) \ 2 = 1.

% fallback: rows without coloured cells keep their input colour
allowed(R,C,Col) :-
    not row_has_color(R),
    input(R,C,Col).

% ----- a colour is allowed for a cell iff it equals the desired colour -----
allowed(R,C,Col) :- desired_color(R,C,Col).

% ----- output cell positions (same size as the input grid) -----
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% ----- exactly one colour per cell, forced to be the allowed one -----
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% ----- reject any colour that is not the allowed one -----
:- output(R,C,Col), not allowed(R,C,Col).
```
rounds.program_before:
% --------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule discovered in the training set:
%   In each row, after the left‑most coloured cell (colour C) the
%   cells to the right are filled by alternating colours:
%       – the immediate neighbour gets the fixed filler colour 5,
%       – the next cell gets the original colour C,
%       – then 5, C, 5, C … alternating.
%   Rows that contain no coloured cell keep their original colour
%   (which is 0 in the given examples).  The filler colour is always
%   5 (as seen in the data).
%
% Encoding strategy:
%   Strategy A (direct derivation).  For every output position we
%   compute exactly one colour that must appear there (the “desired
%   colour”).  The predicate allowed(R,C,V) holds iff V is the
%   desired colour of cell (R,C).  The choice rule
%   1{output(R,C,Col):color(Col)}1:-output_cell(R,C) picks a colour,
%   and the constraint “¬allowed(R,C,Col)” eliminates every colour
%   that is not the desired one.  Because allowed is true for exactly
%   one colour per cell, the solver has no freedom – there is exactly
%   one answer set that matches the reference output.
%
% Edge conditions handled:
%   • Rows without a coloured cell use the fallback clause, so the
%     output colour is the (zero) colour that already appears in the
%     input.
%   • The parity test (C−N) \ 2 = 0 / 1 is written with the safe “\”
%     operator; it works for any integer difference.
%   • The training puzzles never have more than one coloured cell per
%     row, therefore the left‑most coloured cell uniquely determines the
%     whole row.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% ----- grid dimensions (same as the input) -----
max_row(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max { R : row_exists(R) }.
height(H) :- H = 1 + Rmax.

max_col(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max { C : col_exists(C) }.
width(W) :- W = 1 + Cmax.

row_exists(R) :- input(R,_,_).
col_exists(C) :- input(_,C,_).

% ----- leftmost coloured cell in each row -----
first(R,N) :- N = #min { C, Col : input(R,C,Col), Col != 0 }.

has_nonzero(R) :- first(R,N).

orig_color(R,N,Col) :- input(R,N,Col), Col != 0, first(R,N).

% colour that must appear at (R,C) according to the rule
desired_color(R,C,V) :- has_nonzero(R), first(R,N), C < N, V = input(R,C,V).
desired_color(R,C,Col) :- has_nonzero(R), first(R,N), C >= N,
                        (C - N) \ 2 = 0, orig_color(R,N,Col).
desired_color(R,C,5)   :- has_nonzero(R), first(R,N), C >= N,
                        (C - N) \ 2 = 1.
desired_color(R,C,V) :- not has_nonzero(R), input(R,C,V).

% a colour is allowed for a cell iff it equals the desired colour
allowed(R,C,V) :- desired_color(R,C,V).

% output cell positions (same size as the input grid)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% exactly one colour per cell, forced to be the allowed one
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% reject any colour that is not the allowed one
:- output(R,C,Col), not allowed(R,C,Col).

% auxiliary counts (optional, kept for completeness)
nonzero_count(R,N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Fixed Clingo program (only syntax issues addressed)
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% ----- grid dimensions (computed directly, no unsafe vars) -----

width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : col_exists(C) }.

row_exists(R) :- input(R,_,_).
col_exists(C) :- input(_,C,_).

% ----- leftmost coloured cell in each row (N = column index) -----
leftmost(R,N,Col) :-
    N = #min { C : input(R,C,Col2), Col2 != 0 },
    input(R,N,Col),
    Col != 0.

% Row contains at least one coloured cell
row_has_color(R) :- leftmost(R,N,Col).

% ----- desired colour for a cell (safe: N appears positively) -----
% cells left of the leftmost coloured cell keep the original colour
desired_color(R,C,Col) :-
    row_has_color(R),
    leftmost(R,N,Col),
    C < N,
    Col = input(R,C,Col).

% cells right of the leftmost coloured cell:
%   if the distance (C‑N) is even -> colour of the leftmost cell,
%   if the distance is odd   -> filler colour 5
desired_color(R,C,Col) :-
    row_has_color(R),
    leftmost(R,N,Col),
    C >= N,
    (C - N) \ 2 = 0,
    Col = input(R,N,Col).

desired_color(R,C,5) :-
    row_has_color(R),
    leftmost(R,N,Col),
    C >= N,
    (C - N) \ 2 = 1.

% fallback: rows without coloured cells keep their input colour
allowed(R,C,Col) :-
    not row_has_color(R),
    input(R,C,Col).

% ----- a colour is allowed for a cell iff it equals the desired colour -----
allowed(R,C,Col) :- desired_color(R,C,Col).

% ----- output cell positions (same size as the input grid) -----
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% ----- exactly one colour per cell, forced to be the allowed one -----
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% ----- reject any colour that is not the allowed one -----
:- output(R,C,Col), not allowed(R,C,Col).

candidates.prompt:
You are an expert at solving ARC-AGI (Abstraction and Reasoning Corpus) puzzles using Answer Set Programming (ASP) with Clingo.

ARC-AGI puzzles show input-output grid transformations. Each cell has a color value (0–9). Discover the transformation rule from training examples and encode it as an ASP program.

<harness_context>
These are injected automatically — do NOT include them in your program:
  input(Row, Col, Color).   % prepended per example
  color(0..9).              % always present
The output predicate is:  output(Row, Col, Color)
Your program must produce EXACTLY ONE answer set per training example, exactly matching the expected output.
NOTE: Output grid dimensions may differ from input dimensions. Derive them from the transformation rule.
</harness_context>

<clingo_syntax_critical>
Avoid these common mistakes — every one causes a parse or grounding error:

  ✗ aggregate(N = #count, pred(X), N)   →  ✓ N = #count { X : pred(X) }
  ✗ C #mod 2 = 0                        →  ✓ C \ 2 = 0
  ✗ #min { R : p(R) } = Var             →  ✓ Var = #min { R : p(R) }
  ✗ #const MAX = 30.                    →  ✓ #const max_val = 30.
  ✗ { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).
  ✓ 1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- cell(R,C).
  ✗ missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,_).   % unsafe: R ungrounded
  ✓ missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,_).

<encoding_strategies>
Choose the strategy that best fits the puzzle:

STRATEGY A — Direct derivation (use when each output cell is uniquely determined by the rule):
  output(R,C,new_col) :- input(R,C,old_col), maps(old_col, new_col).
  has_color(R,C) :- output(R,C,V), V != 0.
  output(R,C,0) :- output_cell(R,C), not has_color(R,C).    % fill background
  :- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.              % uniqueness

STRATEGY B — Choice + Constraints (use when you need to eliminate wrong candidates):
  1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).
  :- [conditions that eliminate wrong colorings]

For most ARC puzzles, Strategy A is cleaner when the rule directly maps input to output.
</encoding_strategies>

<worked_example>
Puzzle: apply a fixed color substitution (color 3→4, 1→5, 2→6, everything else unchanged).

```asp
% --- Color substitution mapping ---
maps(1,5). maps(2,6). maps(3,4). maps(4,3). maps(5,1). maps(6,2).

% --- Grid dimensions (same as input) ---
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% --- Direct derivation: apply mapping where defined, else keep original color ---
output(R,C,New) :- input(R,C,Old), maps(Old, New).
output(R,C,V)   :- input(R,C,V), not maps(V, _).

% --- Uniqueness constraint ---
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.
```
</worked_example>

% Transformation rule:
% For each row, locate the leftmost non‑zero cell (the anchor).
% Let its column be Acol and its color be AcolCol.
% The output row alternates: cells whose column index has the same parity as Acol keep AcolCol,
% all other cells become 5. Rows without any non‑zero cell stay all 0.
% The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
% Implementation: compute width and height, define an anchor predicate,
% generate candidate colors per cell, and let the solver pick exactly one.

% Dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Anchor: leftmost column with a non‑zero color
row_nonzero_min_col(R,Col) :- Col = #min { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }.

anchor(R,Col,ColCol,ColCol) :- input(R,Col,ColCol), row_nonzero_min_col(R,Col), ColCol != 0.
% Note: the second argument is the column (Col) and the third is the color.
% We reuse the variable names for clarity.

row_has_anchor(R) :- anchor(R,Col,Col,ColCol).

% Candidate colors for each cell
candidate(R,C,V) :- anchor(R,Col,Col,ColCol), V = ColCol, C #mod 2 = Col #mod 2.
candidate(R,C,V) :- anchor(R,Col,Col,ColCol), V = 5, C #mod 2 != Col #mod 2.
candidate(R,C,V) :- not row_has_anchor(R), V = 0.

% Produce exactly one output colour per cell
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V), candidate(R,C,V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Output cell domain
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Find the leftmost non‑zero cell in each row
row_nonzero_min_col(R,Col) :- Col = #min { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }.

% Anchor (leftmost non‑zero column and its colour)
anchor(R,Col,ColCol) :- input(R,Col,ColCol), row_nonzero_min_col(R,Col), ColCol != 0.

% Row has an anchor?
row_has_anchor(R) :- anchor(R,Col,ColCol).

% Candidate colours for each output cell
candidate(R,C,V) :- anchor(R,Col,ColCol), ColCol != 0, C >= Col, C #mod 2 = Col #mod 2, V = ColCol.
candidate(R,C,V) :- anchor(R,Col,ColCol), ColCol != 0, C >= Col, C #mod 2 != Col #mod 2, V = 5.
candidate(R,C,V) :- anchor(R,Col,ColCol), ColCol != 0, C < Col, V = 0.
candidate(R,C,V) :- not row_has_anchor(R), V = 0.

% Exactly one output colour per cell
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V), candidate(R,C,V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Transform rule:
% For each row find the leftmost non‑zero cell (the anchor). Let its column be Ac
% and its colour be AcCol. All cells on or to the right of Ac with the same
% parity as Ac become AcCol, cells with opposite parity become 5, and cells
% left of Ac stay 0. Rows that contain no non‑zero cell stay all 0.
% Output dimensions are equal to the input dimensions.

% Dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Output cell domain
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Leftmost non‑zero column in a row
row_nonzero_min_col(R,Col) :- Col = #min { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }.

% Anchor (column and colour of the leftmost non‑zero cell)
anchor(R,Col,ColCol) :- input(R,Col,ColCol), row_nonzero_min_col(R,Col), ColCol != 0.

% Does the row have an anchor?
row_has_anchor(R) :- anchor(R,Col,ColCol).

% Candidate colour for each output cell
candidate(R,C,V) :- anchor(R,Col,ColCol), ColCol != 0, C >= Col, C #mod 2 = Col #mod 2, V = ColCol.
candidate(R,C,V) :- anchor(R,Col,ColCol), ColCol != 0, C >= Col, C #mod 2 != Col #mod 2, V = 5.
candidate(R,C,V) :- anchor(R,Col,ColCol), ColCol != 0, C < Col, V = 0.
candidate(R,C,V) :- not row_has_anchor(R), V = 0.

% Exactly one colour per cell
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V), candidate(R,C,V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Transform rule: ...
% Dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Output cell domain
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Leftmost non‑zero column
row_nonzero_min_col(R,Col) :- Col = #min { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }.

% Anchor
anchor(R,Col,ColCol) :- input(R,Col,ColCol), row_nonzero_min_col(R,Col), ColCol != 0.

% Row has anchor?
row_has_anchor(R) :- anchor(R,Col,ColCol).

% Candidate colours
candidate(R,C,V) :- anchor(R,Col,ColCol), ColCol != 0, C >= Col, C #mod 2 = Col #mod 2, V = ColCol.
candidate(R,C,V) :- anchor(R,Col,ColCol), ColCol != 0, C >= Col, C #mod 2 != Col #mod 2, V = 5.
candidate(R,C,V) :- anchor(R,Col,ColCol), ColCol != 0, C < Col, V = 0.
candidate(R,C,V) :- not row_has_anchor(R), V = 0.

% Exactly one output colour per cell
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V), candidate(R,C,V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Transformation rule: For each row, locate the leftmost non‑zero cell (the anchor). Let its column be Ac and its colour be AcCol.
% The output row alternates: cells at or to the right of Ac that have the same parity as Ac keep AcCol,
% cells with opposite parity become 5, and cells left of Ac are 0. Rows without any non‑zero cell stay all 0.
% Output dimensions are the same as the input grid.

% Dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Output cell domain

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule (in words):
%   • For each row find the leftmost non‑zero cell – the *anchor*.
%   • Let its column be Ac and its colour be AcCol.
%   • All cells on the right of Ac with the same parity as Ac keep AcCol.
%   • Cells on the right of Ac with opposite parity become 5.
%   • Cells left of Ac stay 0.
%   • Rows without any non‑zero cell contain only 0.
%   • Output dimensions equal the input dimensions.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% Grid dimensions (same as the input)
max_row(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
height(H) :- max_row(Rmax), H = Rmax + 1.

max_col(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
width(W)  :- max_col(Cmax), W = Cmax + 1.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Output domain
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Anchor: leftmost non‑zero cell of a row
row_nonzero_min_col(R,Col) :- Col = #min { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }.

anchor(R,Col,ColCol) :- input(R,Col,ColCol), row_nonzero_min_col(R,Col), ColCol != 0.

row_has_anchor(R) :- anchor(R,Col,ColCol).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Candidate colours for every output cell
candidate(R,C,V) :- anchor(R,Col,ColCol), ColCol != 0, C >= Col, C \ 2 = Col \ 2, V = ColCol.

candidate(R,C,V) :- anchor(R,Col,ColCol), ColCol != 0, C >= Col, C \ 2 != Col \ 2, V = 5.

candidate(R,C,V) :- anchor(R,Col,ColCol), ColCol != 0, C < Col, V = 0.

% If a row has no anchor, the cell must be 0
candidate(R,C,V) :- not row_has_anchor(R), V = 0, output_cell(R,C).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Exactly one colour per cell (the required output)
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V), candidate(R,C,V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

rounds.system_prompt:
You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

# Correct Pattern Reference

```asp
% Computing grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.
```

rounds.response:
```asp
% --------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule (in words):
%   • For each row find the leftmost non‑zero cell – the *anchor*.
%   • Let its column be Ac and its colour be AcCol.
%   • All cells on the right of Ac with the same parity as Ac keep AcCol.
%   • Cells on the right of Ac with opposite parity become 5.
%   • Cells left of Ac stay 0.
%   • Rows without any non‑zero cell contain only 0.
%   • Output dimensions equal the input dimensions.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% Grid dimensions (same as the input)
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_,_) }.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Output domain
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Anchor: leftmost non‑zero cell of a row
row_nonzero_min_col(R,Col) :- Col = #min { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }.

anchor(R,Col,ColCol) :- input(R,Col,ColCol), row_nonzero_min_col(R,Col), ColCol != 0.

row_has_anchor(R) :- anchor(R,Col,ColCol).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Candidate colours for every output cell
candidate(R,C,V) :- anchor(R,Col,ColCol), ColCol != 0,
                C >= Col, C \ 2 = Col \ 2, V = ColCol.
candidate(R,C,V) :- anchor(R,Col,ColCol), ColCol != 0,
                C >= Col, C \ 2 != Col \ 2, V = 5.
candidate(R,C,V) :- anchor(R,Col,ColCol), ColCol != 0,
                C < Col, V = 0.
candidate(R,C,V) :- not row_has_anchor(R), V = 0, R = R.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Exactly one colour per cell (the required output)
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V), candidate(R,C,V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).
```

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule (in words):
%   • For each row find the leftmost non‑zero cell – the *anchor*.
%   • Let its column be Ac and its colour be AcCol.
%   • All cells on the right of Ac with the same parity as Ac keep AcCol.
%   • Cells on the right of Ac with opposite parity become 5.
%   • Cells left of Ac stay 0.
%   • Rows without any non‑zero cell contain only 0.
%   • Output dimensions equal the input dimensions.
% --------------------------------------------------------------
% The program follows the same strategy.
% It computes the grid size, finds the leftmost non‑zero colour in every
% row, and creates a candidate colour for each output cell according to
% the rule described above.  A choice statement then selects exactly one
% colour per cell – this yields a unique answer set that matches the
% expected output.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Grid dimensions (same as the input)
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Output domain
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Anchor: leftmost non‑zero cell of a row
row_nonzero_min_col(R,Col) :- Col = #min { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }.
anchor(R,Col,ColCol)    :- input(R,Col,ColCol), row_nonzero_min_col(R,Col), ColCol != 0.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Row has an anchor (i.e. at least one non‑zero cell)
row_has_anchor(R)      :- anchor(R,Col,ColCol).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Candidate colours for every output cell
candidate(R,C,V) :- anchor(R,Col,ColCol), ColCol != 0,
                C >= Col, C \ 2 = Col \ 2, V = ColCol.
candidate(R,C,V) :- anchor(R,Col,ColCol), ColCol != 0,
                C >= Col, C \ 2 != Col \ 2, V = 5.
candidate(R,C,V) :- anchor(R,Col,ColCol), ColCol != 0,
                C < Col, V = 0.
candidate(R,C,V) :- not row_has_anchor(R), V = 0.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Exactly one colour per cell (the required output)
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V), candidate(R,C,V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

rounds.system_prompt:
You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

# Correct Pattern Reference

```asp
% Computing grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.
```

  aggregate(N=#count,cond,N)    →  N = #count { _ : cond }
  aggregate_all(#count,cond,N)  →  N = #count { _ : cond }
  C #mod 2                      →  C \ 2

  X = #max{...}.  (standalone)  →  max_val(X) :- X = #max{...}.
  #count{A} = #count{B} (body)  →  N = #count{A}, N = #count{B}  (bind one side first)

rounds.system_prompt:
You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

# Correct Pattern Reference

```asp
% Computing grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.
```

```asp
% --------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule (in words):
%   • For each row find the leftmost non‑zero cell – the *anchor*.
%   • Let its column be Ac and its colour be AcCol.
%   • All cells on the right of Ac with the same parity as Ac keep AcCol.
%   • Cells on the right of Ac with opposite parity become 5.
%   • Cells left of Ac stay 0.
%   • Rows without any non‑zero cell contain only 0.
%   • Output dimensions equal the input dimensions.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% Grid dimensions (same as the input)
```
max_row(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
height(H) :- max_row(Rmax), H = Rmax + 1.

max_col(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
width(W) :- max_col(Cmax), W = Cmax + 1.

```asp
% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Output domain
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Anchor: leftmost non‑zero cell of a row
row_nonzero_min_col(R,Col) :- Col = #min { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }.

anchor(R,Col,ColCol) :- input(R,Col,ColCol), row_nonzero_min_col(R,Col), ColCol != 0.

row_has_anchor(R) :- anchor(R,Col,ColCol).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Candidate colours for every output cell
candidate(R,C,V) :- anchor(R,Col,ColCol), ColCol != 0, C >= Col, C \ 2 = Col \ 2, V = ColCol.

candidate(R,C,V) :- anchor(R,Col,ColCol), ColCol != 0, C >= Col, C \ 2 != Col \ 2, V = 5.

candidate(R,C,V) :- anchor(R,Col,ColCol), ColCol != 0, C < Col, V = 0.

% If a row has no anchor, the cell must be 0
candidate(R,C,V) :- not row_has_anchor(R), V = 0, output_cell(R,C).

% -----------------------------------------------------------------
% Exactly one colour per cell (the required output)
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V), candidate(R,C,V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).
```

rewrite_details.system_prompt:
You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

# Correct Pattern Reference

```asp
% Computing grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.
```
